title: Wholemount Immunofluorescence of Organoids (ver 3.0)
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"21vgk","text":"This protocol is for wholemount immunofluorescence of organoids, co-cultures or other 3-dimensional structures that can be grown in scaffolds. Stained organoids are then cleared and imaged with a laser-scanning confocal or lightsheet microscope depending on equipment availability and needs. Optionally, samples can also be cheaply counterstained for amines using the FLARE Protocol (Lee et al., Nature Protocols 2022) before immunostaining is performed. S-phase entry can also be visualised by EdU dosing during the cell culture, and later staining with click detection kits of your choice (the Click-iT EdU are incredibly bright and I prefer them to Ki67 immuno). Cleared samples can be washed after imaging and processed to paraffin if histological characterisation is required. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"bote2","text":"When handling organoids, always pre-coat tips and tubes with 1% BSA solution (or use protein low-bind tubes) to prevent organoids sticking to plastic. Note that for wash steps, ensure that gravity sedimentation or centrifugation are used, and this must be be optimised for each organoid system used to avoid crushing or distorting organoids.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"qkri5","text":"Paraformaldehyde is harmful if inhaled and a suspected carcinogen. Always handle PFA in a fume hood and follow local waste disposal guidelines.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6ahrm","text":"H317 - May cause an allergic skin reaction\nH318 - Causes serious eye damage\nH351 - Suspected of causing cancer","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

You could use lightsheet microscope or spinning disk confocal for quicker data generation (with caveats).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fba7e","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"j8mpu","text":"- 4% Paraformaldehyde (PFA), either premade frozen aliquots or snap-cap image-iT style PFA.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dcg2","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ddq7l","text":"- Borate buffer","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"0mjzw","text":"  200mM (12.36g/L) boric acid in ddH2O, pH to 7.0.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cg7si","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"gyowk","text":"- FLASH reagent 2 (make 10mL) - Messal et al., Nature Protocols 2021 (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33247285/)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"saqga","text":"  200mM borate buffer, pH 7.0","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"e0cqx","text":"  250g/L urea (2.5g per 10mL)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"gdxgt","text":"  80g/L Zwittergent 3-10 (0.8g per 10mL)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8j8jc","text":"  Ensure pH = 7.0","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dsm0f","text":"- PBS","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"de44t","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4m1jg","text":"- Organoid Washing Buffer (OWB)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ppwx9","text":"  PBS","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"hdqja","text":"  0.1% (v/v) Triton X-100","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"a9aoh","text":"  0.2% (w/v) bovine serum albumin","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4eymo","text":"  Store at 4C","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3t2et","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"lnu8e","text":"- TE Buffer (pH 7.5) - 500mL","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"nuydw","text":"  100 mM Tris (Thermo #J65594.A1, CAS 77-86-1, Mw = 121.14 g/mol) - 6.057g Tris","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"e2k39","text":"  0.34 mM EDTA (Sigma #E5134-500G, CAS 6381-92-6, Mw = 372.24 g/mol) - 63.3mg EDTA","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"pxm0f","text":"  pH to 7.5","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"br32c","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"v9pab","text":"- OPTIMUS-Prime refractive-index matching (mounting) buffer as per Kim et al, Exp Mol Med 2025 (https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-025-01550-w)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"l0fgw","text":"  5mL TE buffer (pH 7.5)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7svxu","text":"  75% (w/v) Iohexol (Sigma #D2158, CAS 66108-95-0) - 2.402g Urea","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"x1p0f","text":"  Heat to 60C with occasional inversion to mix (be patient, there is a lot of iohexol in here to dissolve)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5kjxk","text":"  Once dissolved, make up to 10mL volume with TE buffer.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"o67lw","text":"  Store at 4C.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"27rho","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ngaoa","text":"- Primary and Secondary Antibodies - choose fluorophores carefully to reduce spectral overlap","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"75kau","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bt45b","text":"- Optional: Invitrogen Click-iT EdU Cell Proliferation Kit for Imaging (I use Alexafluor 555)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8bh42","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9q3va","text":"- Optional: MES Buffer (pH 6.0) - if using amine counterstain for morphology","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"h8vya","text":"  25mL of ddH2O","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"rwxgb","text":"  586mg MES (2-(N-Morpholino)ethanesulfonic acid hydrate)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"zmniv","text":"  263mg NaCl","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"evryh","text":"  pH to 6 using 1M NaOH","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"jnien","text":"  Store at RT (~2 weeks or until precipitate appears, then discard and make fresh)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"e0i5i","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"0bggs","text":"- Optional: AZDye-647-NHS-ester (2BScientific #FP-1121-1MG) - if using amine counterstain","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"as6qv","text":"  Make a 5mg/mL stock solution in DMSO","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"gj4a3","text":"  Make small aliquots (1-5uL) and store at -20C","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Collect specimens (organoids, 3D cultures etc) as you normally would, taking best efforts to keep them intact. This varies depending on application and the type of culture. **CRITICAL If you are handling organoids with tips, following the recommendation in Dekkers’ paper and coat them in sterile 1% BSA in PBS by pipetting 1mL a few times before you touch any organoids. Use protein low-bind (or BSA-precoated) tubes to collect them. This will stop them sticking, which WILL happen once they’re released from matrigel!
For organoids that must be taken out of BME/Matrigel scaffolds, recommended harvest is on ice-cold Corning Cell Recovery solution, rocking, for up to 01:00:00 On ice .

Collect organoids at the bottom of an eppendorf tube by gravity sedimentation or gentle centrifugation. Aspirate supernatant and fix in >1ml or 10 volumes of cold 4% PFA for 00:45:00 minutes at On ice (on ice). Warning: PFA is harmful if inhaled. Ensure PFA is added and removed in a fume hood.

Centrifuge/sediment organoids. Wash with OWB (2x) 00:05:00 If staining is to be conducted on another day, the organoids can now be kept at 4 °C for short term storage, and the protocol can be resumed with part, or all, of the organoids at a later date.

Make up FLASH reagent 2 freshly (I make up 10mL) and keep at 37 °C in a water bath until use. Add 2.5g urea and 0.8g Zwittergent-3-10 into 10mL 200 millimolar (mM) sodium borate buffer, pH 7.0 If storing excess FLASH 2 buffer for later use, store remainder in the fridge and warm up to 37 °C again to re-dissolve.
Aspirate excess wash buffer and add 1mL FLASH 2 reagent per tube. Pack tubes into a rocker or nutator with gentle agitation and incubate at 37 °C for 02:00:00 .
Wash twice with PBS to remove excess FLASH 2 reagent (2x) 00:05:00

Optional: At this point, organoids can be stained with AZDye-647-NHS-ester to detect cellular amines (mostly proteins). This visualises general morphological characteristics more distinctly than phalloidin. Add 0.5uL of dye stock per 1mL of MES buffer, pH 6.0. Incubate with rocking at room temperature for 02:00:00 , then wash twice with MES buffer 00:05:00 before proceeding.
Wash with OWB, (3x) 00:10:00 with constant rotation.

Prepare primary antibody solutions by diluting stock antibodies in OWB. You can mix e.g. a mouse and a rabbit primary antibody into the cocktail for multiplexing. You can get away with 200uL per tube as long as samples are mixed well, but more is better (recommended volume for large organoids is 500uL). Dilutions that work for standard IHC on paraffin normally work well here too in my experience, but further titration might be needed to reduce background staining depending on the antibody.
Aspirate wash buffer carefully. A small amount of residual liquid is fine. Add antibody dilutions.
Incubate on a rotator at room temperature Overnight for organoids or two days at 4 °C for large co-cultures. In our experience, room temperature incubations improve uniformity of labelling.
Wash antibodies out with one brief rinse of OWB, then 3x10 min washes with constant rotation.

Prepare secondary antibody solutions by diluting stock antibodies in OWB. You can mix e.g. an anti-mouse and an anti-rabbit secondary antibody into the cocktail for multiplexing. Prepare 200-500uL per sample. Normally 1:500-1:1000 are reasonable dilutions for Molecular Probes alexafluor secondaries, but further titration might be needed depending on the antibody and the presence of background signal. At this point you can also add DAPI (350/405nm excitation) at 1:500 or methyl green (633nm excitation) at 1:500 for nuclear counterstaining. Ensure that the fluorophores are spectrally distinct to allow appropriate excitation and detection later with minimal bleedthrough, and that your fluorophores can be excited by the lasers on the microscope you choose to use (I recommend AlexaFluors 488, 568 and 633, the further apart the better).
Add secondary antibodies to specimen and incubate at room temperature with constant rotation Overnight
Wash antibodies out with one brief rinse of OWB, then (3x) 00:10:00 washes with constant rotation.

Optional: Proceed with EdU detection if a Click-iT EdU detection kit (or similar copper click reaction) is being used and cells have been dosed before fixation as below. Copper salts used in conventional click detection kits will interfere with some other reagents used in immunolabelling, such as phalloidin binding (phalloidin hates copper). Always do this reaction after other detection steps and optimise copper sulfate concentrations carefully if phalloidin is a necessity.
Optional: Prepare click detection reaction according to manufacturer's directions, allowing 200-500uL of click reaction per tube. Incubate with the sample for 30 minutes at room temperature.
Wash 2x with OWB 00:10:00 and proceed to clearing.

Remove as much OWB as possible, and add 200uL OPTIMUS-prime clearing buffer. Apply constant rotation for at least 30 minutes at room temperature, verifying that organoids are cleared with an epifluorescence microscope prior to imaging on a confocal microscope. Do not use excessive amounts of clearing buffer as organoids will be difficult to see at this point. Pause point: samples can be kept at 4C for short term storage prior to imaging.

Mount samples using your preferred method. Pipetting up and down with a cut yellow tip will allow you to clearly see the organoids (they will be translucent but just about visible in the clearing solution when mixed, and usually at the bottom of the tube). I 3D print a ~0.4mm height square mounting frame in PLA, which I stick to glass slides using clear nail varnish. I then add all mounted organoids in, add a 22mm square coverslip, and seal the edges with more nail varnish. I have also had success with stacked adhesive reinforcement rings (as you would find in a folder), 50uL sample volume, and a circular 16mm coverslip, sealed with nail varnish. Ibidi chamber slides could also be used. Each lab will have different needs depending on their sample: consult with an imaging specialist if unsure what is suitable for the sample and microscope in question. Samples are now ready for imaging.

Image on a point-scanning confocal, spinning-disk confocal or light sheet microscope as appropriate. I use a Leica SP8 laser-scanning confocal microscope with a 20x long working distance glycerol immersion objective. Long working distance objectives are important: ideally immersion objectives with immersion liquid that is close to the RI of the mounting media (OPTIMUS-Prime RI=1.47), but 63x can be used for high-resolution imaging with a lower volume/lower working distance.
Following imaging, if processing for FFPE is desired, clearing buffer can be washed out with three changes of PBS, 10 minutes each.
Embed samples in molten 2% (w/v) agarose in PBS (cooled to <60C), let cool, and cut to size where required. Store agarose blocks in a 5mL bijoux filled with 70% ethanol.
Process to FFPE in standard histology casettes, using a tissue processor as standard.
creator: Benjamin Sharpe
